# Online Optimisers - New Capabilities Content Block

**Purpose:** Reusable copy describing the shift from "local SEO agency" to full one-stop-shop. Drops into any deck, email, handover, or sales call when you need the "here is what I can do for you now" story.

**Status:** Live, v1, 2026-04-08
**Tone:** Friendly founder, confident, not pitchy. Written in Donal's voice.

---

title: One-line version
content:
Online Optimisers is now a true one-stop-shop for your local business growth: brand, website, content, SEO, AI visibility, ads, reviews, lead nurture, and automation, all run by one small expert team using a central AI brain.

---

title: 50-word version (email / opener)
content:
Over the last month I've quietly rebuilt what Online Optimisers can do for you. Instead of hiring five different agencies (branding, SEO, ads, web, automation), you now have one team, one point of contact, and one AI central brain running everything together. Faster, cheaper, more joined up.

---

title: 100-word version (deck close slide, video intro)
content:
A few years ago a serious local business needed five vendors to grow online: a brand designer, a content writer, an SEO agency, an ads team, and someone to glue it all together. That's slow, expensive, and nothing talks to anything else.

Online Optimisers is now the opposite. One team. One operator (me, with my crew). One AI central brain that ties brand, content, SEO, ads, reviews, nurture, and automation into a single system. Your website, your rankings, your ads, your reviews, and your lead follow-up all pulling in the same direction.

---

title: Full capability list (grid / bullet use)
content:
**1. Branding**
Logo, color system, voice guidelines, visual identity refresh.

**2. Website design and development**
Modern Astro + EmDash builds on Cloudflare. Fast, secure, AI-ready, built for conversions.

**3. Hosting and infrastructure**
Cloudflare Workers, D1 database, R2 media storage. Global CDN speed, enterprise-grade uptime, sensible pricing.

**4. Content strategy and production**
Long-form articles, service pages, city pages, review-rich content designed for both Google and LLM visibility.

**5. Local SEO**
Google Business Profile management, local keyword tracking, service pages, location pages, schema, citations, review workflow. The core retainer.

**6. AI visibility / LLM ranking**
The new service. Getting your business cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews, and Gemini. Entity building, schema, knowledge graph, Wikidata.

**7. Link building and authority**
White-hat backlinks, directory citations, niche edits, PR-style placements.

**8. Meta ads (Facebook + Instagram)**
Campaign strategy, creative, setup, optimization. Lead form integration with your CRM.

**9. Google ads (Search + Local Services Ads)**
Keyword strategy, ad copy, landing pages, conversion tracking, bid management.

**10. Review and reputation management**
Automated review requests, review response workflows, reputation monitoring, Google/Yelp/industry site aggregation.

**11. Lead nurture and follow-up**
Automated SMS + email sequences, missed-call text-back, web chat to SMS. Leads never go cold.

**12. Sales automation**
CRM workflows, pipeline management, booking link integration, proposal delivery. Done inside GHL or integrated with your existing system.

**13. CRM and workflow integration**
If you use Supermove, JobNimbus, Dentrix, Housecall Pro, or any other industry CRM, we can integrate it into the central system so leads, jobs, and customers flow through one place.

**14. AI automation (the central brain)**
Custom AI workflows that sit across your whole business: inbox triage, quote drafting, reporting, review sentiment analysis, content generation. This is what lets one small team run everything without dropping the ball.

---

title: The "Old Way vs New Way" story (infographic voiceover)
content:
### The old way

A serious local business needed:
- A branding agency for the logo and identity
- A content agency for the blog
- An SEO agency for Google rankings
- A Facebook ads agency for social
- A Google ads agency for paid search
- A web dev agency for the site
- A hosting company for servers
- A lead nurture tool vendor
- A review management tool vendor
- Someone to tie it all together (usually the owner, at midnight, badly)

Five to ten different vendors. None of them talked to each other. The owner ended up as the project manager even though they were supposed to be running the business.


### The new way

One operator, one small expert team, one AI central brain. Brand, content, SEO, AI visibility, ads, reviews, nurture, automation, CRM integration, web, hosting, all unified. One point of contact. One invoice. One strategy. Everything moves in the same direction because everything is run from the same brain.

---

title: What NOT to say
content:
- Don't say "digital marketing" or "marketing agency" - too generic, spam-word territory for emails.
- Don't say "scale" or "growth hacking" or "optimise" - spam triggers, per the cold email rules in `.claude/rules/07-cold-email.md`.
- Don't say "guaranteed results" - AI visibility especially is probabilistic, we don't guarantee.
- Don't position CRM integration as a requirement - it's an option. Aspirational, client-appropriate.
- Don't talk about the OO team (Niti, Meghana, Francis, Angelie, Tanatsa) by name in client-facing copy unless the client already knows them. Keep it "I and my team."

title: Untitled
content:
# Tomas Sales System

Donal's brother and 50/50 business partner. Handles sales calls. Capacity: 4-5 calls/day.

---

title: Pipeline
content:
```
lead → audit → VSL → outreach → call → close
```

1. **Lead** - sourced from cold email (Instantly/Bison), LinkedIn, or referral
2. **Audit** - quick AI ranking audit generated via /quick-audit (5-10 min)
3. **VSL** - personalised video generated and deployed to landing page
4. **Outreach** - email sent via Instantly/Bison with GIF thumbnail + VSL link
5. **Call** - Tomas books and runs the sales call (Fathom records)
6. **Close** - proposal sent, GHL CRM updated, onboarding triggered

---

title: CRM Rule
content:
**Every lead must have a `next_action` field set.** No lead sits in limbo.

Use GHL pipeline stages:
- `new` - just entered
- `audited` - audit done, VSL pending or sent
- `contacted` - outreach sent, awaiting reply
- `call_booked` - call in calendar
- `call_done` - debrief needed
- `proposal_sent` - awaiting decision
- `closed_won` - client onboarded
- `closed_lost` - record objections, set follow-up date

---

title: Call Debrief Template
content:
After every call, paste notes into Claude with this format and Alex will extract + update the log:

```
CALL DEBRIEF
Date: [date]
Prospect: [name, business, city]
Outcome: [booked / no show / interested / not interested / closed]

Pain: [what problem did they describe?]
Objections: [what did they push back on?]
Signals: [what lit them up? what language did they use?]
Mistakes: [anything that went wrong or could improve?]
Next move: [exactly what happens next]
```

---

title: Objection Library
content:
Updated after each call. Seed list below - grow over time.

| Objection | Category | Best response angle |
|---|---|---|
| "We already do SEO" | Existing service | Differentiate AI ranking vs traditional SEO - different buyers in Google vs AI answers |
| "What results have you got?" | Social proof | Case studies + specific movers client examples |
| "Too expensive" | Price | Reframe as lead gen system cost vs 1 new client value |
| "I need to think about it" | Stall | Ask what specific part they need to think about - isolate the real objection |
| "We get most leads from referrals" | No need | AI ranking adds a new channel, doesn't replace referrals |
| "I tried SEO before and it didn't work" | Bad experience | Acknowledge, then explain why AI ranking is different (different search behaviour) |

---

title: Hook Library (best opening lines by niche)
content:
Movers:
- "I searched [city] movers in ChatGPT and you didn't come up - your competitor did."
- "Most moving companies I check aren't showing up in AI search at all yet."

Roofers:
- "I looked up emergency roof repair in [city] in Perplexity - three companies came up, none of them were you."

---

title: Performance Log
content:
| Date | Calls | Booked | Closed | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| - | - | - | - | Start tracking from next call batch |

title: The Frog (today's #1 priority)
content:
**Updated daily.** Today: _set during morning brief_

Format: `<verb> <thing> <by when> <because>`

Examples of frogs that count:
- "Run /quick-audit on Glow To Go and ship the audit deck by EOD because the proposal is waiting"
- "Close out the 3 stalled lead follow-ups by 3pm because they fall off after 72hrs"
- "Ship the AI ranking agent v0.1 stub by lunch because Khaled Phase 3 lands next week and we need the shell ready"

title: Decision Filters (the rules every agent applies)
content:
When agents propose actions, they pass through these filters in order:

1. **Revenue Brain:** does this move revenue up, protect it, or save time worth money? If no → don't escalate to Donal.
2. **Cashflow priority:** retainer client work > sales support > systematisation > internal tooling.
3. **2-hour rule:** anything that takes >2hr before evidence/revenue gets challenged ("what are you dropping to make room?").
4. **Eat the frog:** if there's a task Donal is avoiding, that goes first.
5. **Frog vs Blocker separation:** 1 frog/day, blockers handled in batched windows.
6. **80/20 lens:** which 20% of effort gets 80% of the result?
7. **First principles:** when stuck, strip back to "what are we actually trying to accomplish?"

---

title: Escalation Triggers (only these reach Donal)
content:
- Spend > $50/day not pre-approved
- New client decision (close, refuse, refund)
- Hire or fire decision
- Strategy pivot (new offer, new channel)
- Anything client-facing that involves real outreach (sandbox safe)
- Any decision a Council member flags ESCALATE

Everything else: agents handle, log to daily brief, Donal reads in the morning.

title: Platform Decision

title: Campaign 1 — Movers Niche (Mirror Instantly)
content:
**Platform:** Bison
**Niche:** Moving companies (US)
**Target:** Same geo/niche as existing Instantly campaigns (movers)
**Sequence:** Mirror the existing 3-step Instantly sequence

Step 1 (Day 0):
```
Subject: {city} movers search result

Hey {{firstName}},

I looked up {{city}} movers in ChatGPT and your company didn't come up - one of your competitors did.

I put together a quick breakdown showing exactly what's missing and how to fix it.

Worth a look?

[GIF thumbnail - links to VSL]
```

Step 2 (Day 3 bump):
```
Subject: Re: {city} movers search result

Did you get a chance to see this {{firstName}}?
```

Step 3 (Day 6 new angle):
```
Subject: while you're busy moving people...

...your competitor is stealing the AI search results.

[VSL landing page link]

Happy to show you how to flip this. Just reply yes.
```

**Goal:** A/B test Bison vs Instantly reply rates on identical sequence over 2 weeks.

title: Campaign 3 — VSL Reply Trigger (Automation)

**Platform:** Bison webhook → n8n → Claude

**Action:**

title: Untitled
content:
# Google Sheets Formatting Preferences

The standard for any one-off Google Sheet created for Online Optimisers. Every sheet must look like a product, not a data dump. If Tomas opens it and it looks rough, he won't use it.

---

title: Color Palette
content:
| Name | Hex | Use |
|------|-----|-----|
| Navy | #061B3F | Header rows, section headers, tab color |
| Mid-navy | #253571 | Subtitle rows, secondary headers |
| Accent blue | #3498DB | Accent text on dark backgrounds |
| Light blue tint | #F0F3FC | Alternating row stripe (even rows) |
| White | #FFFFFF | Alternating row stripe (odd rows), main bg |
| Amber light | #FFF3CC | Critical / warning section tint |
| Green light | #E0FAE9 | Success / confirmed section tint |
| Blue light | #D1EAF9 | Info / data section tint |
| Cream | #FFFFF8 | Notes / voice dump column background |
| Orange | #F5A434 | Critical section header |
| Green header | #4DBB7F | Positive / success section header |

---

title: Header Row
content:
- Navy background (#061B3F), white bold text
- Font: Inter (fall back to Arial), 10pt
- Center-aligned horizontally, middle-aligned vertically
- Wrap text ON — headers should break to 1-2 lines, not expand the column
- Height: 50-55px
- Frozen (always visible when scrolling)
- Filters enabled across all columns

**Header length rule:** Keep headers SHORT. Max ~6 characters for boolean/number columns. Abbreviate aggressively: "ChatGPT Cited" → "GPT", "Domain Rank" → "DR", "Google My Business" → "GMB", "Website Status" → "Site Up". If the header wraps into unreadable gibberish, it is broken.

---

title: Column Widths
content:
| Column type | Width |
|-------------|-------|
| Boolean (Y/N, checkboxes) | 45-55px |
| Score / rating / number | 55-75px |
| Short status | 75-90px |
| Name / business name | 130-160px |
| City / niche / short text | 90-120px |
| URL / link | 250-280px |
| Notes / justification | 300-400px |
| Voice dump / long text | 350-450px |

Never wider than necessary. Every column earns its width. Data should be scannable without horizontal scrolling where possible.

---

title: Data Rows
content:
- Font: Inter (Arial fallback), 9pt, dark text (#1A1D26)
- Zebra stripes: white (#FFFFFF) odd rows, light blue (#F0F3FC) even rows
- Vertical align: middle
- Wrap text ON for notes columns, OFF for data columns

---

title: Conditional Formatting
content:
| Condition | Style |
|-----------|-------|
| Priority HIGH / CRITICAL | Red background + red text |
| Priority MEDIUM / AMBER | Amber background + amber text |
| Priority LOW / GREEN | Green background + green text |
| Lead rating A | Green background |
| Lead rating B | Blue background |
| Lead rating C | Amber background |
| Lead rating D | Red background |
| Lead rating F | Dark red background + white text |
| YES / TRUE | Green text |
| NO / FALSE | Red text |
| NOINDEX | Bright red bg + white bold text |
| Status: Done | Green text |
| Status: In Progress | Blue text |
| Status: Needs Fix | Red text |
| Dead / dropped rows | Full row: light pink bg + muted gray text |

---

title: Section Headers (within a tab)
content:
Used when a tab mixes summary sections with data rows (e.g. a README-style tab or dashboard):

- Merged across all columns
- Navy background (#061B3F), accent blue text (#3498DB), bold 11pt
- Variants: orange (#F5A434) for CRITICAL, green (#4DBB7F) for success/positive
- Content rows below a section header get the matching tint (amber/green/blue)
- Bold the label column in content rows

---

title: Dropdowns (Data Validation)
content:
- Always use ONE_OF_LIST with showCustomUi: true
- Standard status options: In Progress / Needs Fix / Done
- Standard sent options: Yes / No
- Boolean fields: Yes / No (not TRUE/FALSE — humans read it)

---

title: Tab Structure
content:
- Color-code tabs: navy (#061C3F) for main data tabs, green for status/health tabs, amber for attention-needed tabs
- Always include a README or Dashboard tab if the sheet has 3+ data tabs
- README tab: merged title row (navy, 16pt white bold), subtitle row (mid-navy, accent text), then color-coded sections

---

title: Callout Boxes / Insight Rows
content:
- Gold/cream background (#FEF9C3) for key insight callouts
- Wrap text, italic for emphasis in insight text
- Bold the label cell

---

title: Charts
content:
- Dark background (#0F1C3F or #1A1D26) for chart area
- Inter font on all chart labels
- Line charts: bright accent blue (#3498DB) lines
- Stacked bar: use orange/blue/green to distinguish models or categories
- Always add chart titles
- Chart width: 600-700px, height: 300-400px

---

title: API Implementation Notes
content:
When applying formatting programmatically (batchUpdate via Sheets API):

- Use `repeatCell` for background colors, text formatting, number formats
- Use `mergeCells` with `MERGE_ALL` for section headers
- Use `updateDimensionProperties` for column widths and row heights
- Use `addConditionalFormatRule` with `CUSTOM_FORMULA` type (not `NUMBER_GREATER_EQ` - that is invalid)
- Conditional formula example: `=AND($A2<>"", $C2="Yes")` - always reference the full row anchor
- OAuth tokens at `C:/Users/nexto/.config/google-drive-mcp/tokens.json` — use refresh pattern in format scripts
- Reference working scripts: `c:/tmp/format-usage-sheet.py` (full nav theme + charts) and `c:/tmp/format-readme.py` (README tab pattern + section colors)

---

title: Standard Tab Set for Deal/Lead Sheets
content:
Per the Safe4USA gold standard (sheet ID `1wRif8WvhxduP68kVqzLEW7zrjC-pb4tP1EdqjvzmJK0`):
- Tab 1: Dashboard (headline metrics, status, key links)
- Tab 2: Website Pages (page audit data)
- Tab 3: Timeline (milestones, delivery schedule)

Full column + color spec locked in `.claude/skills/lead-brief.md` ("Google Sheet Formatting - Hard Spec" section). Working tooling: `c:/tmp/handyman-sheet-format.py` and `c:/tmp/handyman-sheet-tabs.py`.

title: Sales ABC Cheat Sheet
content:
The ABC Cheat Sheet 
 
5 ways to ask for the sale:  
 
1. Get the prospect so excited about your product, they finally ask you, "How much 
does this cost?!" 
 
2. "Let's get it a shot!"  
Casual, but gets the prospect to bring up the purchase.  
 
3. "Why don't we get started?" 
Direct. Effective.  
 
4. "It sounds like I've answered all of your questions. Let's sign you up!" 
Great if you've been talking to your prospect for a while, and answered all of his 
questions.  
 
5. "Will you pay with MasterCard or Visa today?" 
I love this one. I ask it when the prospect knows the price and is hot and ready to 
buy. Surprisingly, they will most likely respond with the CC number :)  
 
***Keep in mind***​
 It's OKAY if your prospect responds "No!"... Or throws and 
objection at you. This gives you the opportunity to ask "Why?" And continue the 
conversation.  
 
I've heard sales reps ask 8 to 10 times for the sale on ONE call!!! And guess what 
happened? The prospect finally caved in and bought the product.  
 
In sales, someone is always going to be closed. It's either your prospect...Or YOU 
because the prospect sold you on why they can't buy! 
 
So which side are you on? 
 
Ask for the sale and always always A.B.C.

title: DFY Agency Sales Call Structure
content:
AGENCY CALLS SALES SCRIPT 
The following is a 2-call sales process. 2 calls allow you time to qualify the lead, strategize, and audit their ad accounts to further build authority. 
Call #1 
Establish rapport (Talk like a friend) 
Frame the call 
Start the conversation with open ended question 
Identify pain points 
Identify Client’s Goals
Get verbal commitment 
Establish next steps 
Request ad account access 
Establish rapport 
Try to build a real connection with your lead. Thing of this as an ice breaker and it can be really simple. Tell them something funny that happened earlier in the day. Ask them how their day is going and if you know where they are calling from, comment on the location.
Example: Are you calling from Atlanta? Oh I love it up there, I have some family there and travel there a few times a year. (Small talk, make a connection) 
Frame the call 
“Okay James, so we’ve got about half hour together and what I want to do is establish whether or not we are a good fit to help you. I’m going to ask you a few questions about your business, and advertising goals, and then I’ll share more about exactly what we do and from there we’ll be able to see if it’s a good fit for both of us. Sounds good? Great!
Start with an open-ended question
“So why don’t you start by telling me a little bit more about your business - how long have you been running FB ads for now?” 

Identify problem/issue 
I HIGHLY recommend you have some kind of qualification form that leads have to fill out before booking a call with you. If you have this you can head off this spot by asking about the issues the lead is facing. Make sure you ask about what is going wrong and write down all of the pain points you can.
On our qualification form (we use Wufoo) we ask:
What are you goals in 3 months of working with us?
Why do you feel you haven’t been able to achieve those goals already?
What is the main reason you have not been able to achieve your revenue target?
These questions help you identify pain points and goals before you get on the phone with a lead, but if you have not already asked them, make sure you do on the phone and get these pain points written down in the exact words that they use to describe them. 
If you can compare their issues to mini case study you have or make a client comparison here that is HUGE! 
Identify Client’s Goals 
“We don’t work with a ton of clients and make each one of the just a number. Every one of our clients works in close contact with us to achieve specific goals laid out in the beginning of our contracts. What would need to happen in your business in the next six months for you to leave us a raving testimonial?” 
Here you want to help them dreamscape - what outcomes are they desiring, what are the revenue and financial targets they’re aiming for? 
This is a good place to ask them about their manufacturing capacity and compare to another one of your clients they could relate to. 
“Those are some great goals. It’s a similar path another one of our clients took back in May. They started with us in the same position and by August we had scaled them to X”
“As I mentioned before, we only onboard clients we know for 100% certain we can knock it out of the park for. If we do move forward here, are you prepared for the influx of sales that would occur to reach your revenue goal of X? Facebook ads can really suffer in performance if we have to shut them off due to an out-of-stock situation” 
GET CRUCIAL INFORMATION:
What is your current CPA? Target CPA?
What is your Current AOV? Target AOV?
What is your Website Conversion Rate?
What are your Revenue Goals 3 months from now? 6 months from now?
Tell the lead all about what you do, how you do it and why you’re the best. DO NOT TEACH or go into techno-mumble-jumble. They will tune out if you do this. 
Set Up Next Call
“So it sounds like we might be able to help you here. What’s going to happen next is I am going to take all of this information back to my team. I’m not the one who is running the ads, so I want to confirm with my team, map out your full strategy and run some calculations to get some expected ad spends that match your revenue goals. Are you near your computer? It’s best that we get analyst access to your FB ad account that way we can audit it and see where you’re at currently. Analyst access just means we can see the data, but not change it.”
 Get analyst access to FB ads account. Do this on the phone with them and guide them through the approval process.
Wrap Up Call 
“Perfect! Ok so please give us about a day or 2 to audit the account and get your strategy all mapped out. In the meantime, feel free to shoot me an email with any questions!” 
Call #2
Re-establish rapport 
Recap objectives/goals 
Recap problems/issues 
Share audit findings 
Explain solution 

Stack up 
Pitch 
Next steps 
Take deposit / set follow up reminder 
R-establish Rapport 
Reconnect with the lead. Tell them something funny that just happened to you. Draw from the last call to find common interest or conversational topic. Just be FRIENDLY 
Recap Client Goals + Pain Points
“Okay so last time we spoke you mentioned that you were looking to scale to 50K-100K month at a CPA of $14 dollars BUT you have been finding that some of your products are very dependent on the time of year and right now your Facebook ads are only breaking even”

When recapping their pain points, use the EXACT language that they used when describing it to you on call #1
Share audit findings 
Example: “So my team audited your ad account and we found several things. First, you’re using your personal ad account, moving forward we would need to set up a business ad account. We also noticed you are also running 2 pixels on your site, and neither of them are connected to a product catalog. We need to find if either pixel has any valuable data get that connected to your new business ad account. Next, there was virtually no audience testing in your ads, and no retargeting either. There is a ton of money being left on the table here we could recoup pretty quickly when we launch retargeting” 
Explain Solution 
Here is where you detail how your services can help them and how you would do it. Don’t get TOO technical, don’t teach, just explain the strategy with confidence. 
Explain Your Pricing Structure:
Example: “How we onboard clients in your position is an initial 3- month agreement. Month 1 is a setup fee to get the funnel built and months 2 and 3 are flat retainers while we scale you to your revenue and CPA targets.”
Stack Up 
“Now, our goal here is to become your long-term partner, because the more you grow the more we grow and we definitely see potential here. So you’re looking to make an initial 50-100K a month, and over the course of 6 months we would be looking at 350-500K, which will take some heavy scaling from you current position. But after reviewing everything here and based on past clients in a similar situation, we are confident we can make this happen for you.
Pitch 
So the cost to set everything up will be just 5K and we would require a 3-month commitment at 3K, how does that work for you guys? 
It’s important that you DON’T SPEAK after the above line! This can create awkward silence but just grit through it while they work through the buyer consideration phase. 
Next Steps 
Great! Ok so here’s what’s going to happen. I’m going to send you over the Terms of Service Agreement for you to review and sign. The sooner you can fill this in the sooner we can start building your funnel and campaigns. The TOS agreement has everything we have already spoke about just in greater detail. We can’t wait to get started, welcome!”

title: Rank Raptor - Gatekeeper Rebuttals
content:
Gate-Keeper Rebuttals
Getting their guard down










Gate-Keeper Rebuttals
Getting Past the Secretary


A gatekeeper is what the sales world refers to as the secretary or the receptionist that picks up the phone calls, instead of the owner. 

Gate keepers are trained to turn away sales calls, so it is VITAL that we don’t pitch to the gatekeepers! All we want to do is gently request to speak to someone who is either the owner or decision maker, and there are various ways to go about doing so. 

Be courteous and say “Please”...a lot. 
Never pitch your service/product to the gatekeeper, they don’t care. 
Be prepared for pushback statements, they don’t want you to pitch anything to their boss. 
First ask to speak to their boss by name, if not possible, ask for the decision maker’s personal contact number or email. If you get pushback, then ask to schedule an appointment to speak with the decision maker, and if you get pushback there, ask to just leave a message. 
Sound like you have authority. Receptionists listen to orders and instruction, its their job, so sound like you are on the same authority as their boss, maybe a colleague or someone that knows them, and you are way more likely to get through. 








Gatekeeper Openers

Receptionist: Hello this is Janice from DC Plumbing, how may I help you?

You: Hey Janice, hope you’re doing good today, May I speak to John please? You can tell him Kris is holding please. 
-----------------------------------------------

Receptionist: Hello this is Janice from DC Plumbing, how may I help you?

You: Hey Janice, I’d like you to put me through to John, tell him Kris from JJ solutions is calling.
----------------------------------------------

Receptionist: Hello this is Janice from DC Plumbing, how may I help you?

You: Hey Janice I hope you’re doing well today, John and I have been playing phone tag the last few days, would you mind putting me through to him? You can Tell him Kris is holding.
----------------------------------------------









Gatekeeper Pushback Statements
Rebuttals


“What is the call in regards to” 
A) I have a mass job project for [DECISIONMAKER], just calling to get in touch with him or schedule a formal call as there’s nitty-gritty details and questions to discuss.  
B) I have a pretty big project laid out for [DECISIONMAKER], and I’d like to get a 15-20 minute formal call with him. 
C) I have a high-tier job that needs to be done, and my assistant recommended I get in touch with you guys. Would you be able to connect me with a project manager or decision maker? 




“Is this a sales call”
A) Yes and no, while I genuinely have a job or two for you guys right now, I also have referrals for roofing jobs coming to me on a daily basis and I wanted to partner with a roofing company in Atlanta to take them on. 
B) Its a sales call and an inquiry for a new roof. I have a client that needs a whole roof replacement, but I also have dozens of clients per month that need the same, so you can decide if its a sales call, I just want to know if I can speak to the main project manager. 


“He does not take sales calls”
A) Well I wouldn’t label this a sales call, as I have a few jobs ready for him to take on. 
B) I’m not a salesman, just a middle man that has clients for him. I’m sorry if I came off like a sales telemarketer, I didn’t pick your company out of a hat, I am meaning to get in touch with you. 




“He will not be interested in that”
A) I understand you may think that, but my secretary just turned away a gentlemen that I was expecting to call me the other day by saying the exact same thing. I just want to ask him directly if he’s interested or not.
B) I appreciate your opinion, but I would feel a lot better if I heard that directly from him.



“We are already using someone”
A) That’s concerning because they are obviously missing a huge market that I am hitting with minimal effort. 
B) May I ask how many leads they bring you per month? 
C) Are you happy with how much they are charging you?



“Can you send me your information”
A) I sure can, however there’s some industry-technical jargon in there, so could you give me the contact to the owner or a decision maker? 
B) Of course, however there is some mid-sensitive information about my business and my clients in there, so would it be possible to get the owner’s direct email address? I just would prefer if that information wasn’t forwarded and CC’d to people other than my intended person of interest.

title: Kyle Adams - Backlinks & Citations
content:
Google uses both backlinks and citations to determine the authority your website has. 
The higher authority your website has, the higher it ranks in Google searches. 
Backlink 
• 
A link to your site from another site 
• 
Can be a text link, a picture, etc. 
• 
The more authority the website has that is linking to your site, the better the backlink 
• 
Number of/Quality of your backlinks are one of the heaviest weighing factors for your 
organic Google ranking 
Citation 
• 
Online reference to your business Name, Address, Phone Number (NAP) 
• 
Does not need to be linked to your website to get credit 
• 
Mainly used for Google map pack rankings (you’ll learn more about this in the GMB 
lesson in Optimization Pt. 2) 
Please note: This is a SUPER SIMPLE and SUPER BRIEF overview of these 
topics. We will deep dive into them later in the course!

title: Kyle Adams - Getting Paid
content:
You’ve got leads, now how do you get paid? 
 
Two main ways: 
1. Credit Card – Use Stripe, Paypal, or Square. You will get charged a processing fee 
(typically 3%), but the ability to set up auto-pay makes this a no brainer. 
2. Cash – Money in hand is great, but it makes it difficult to track expenses and income for 
tax purposes. It’s nice to have a few cash clients on the side, but I’d never encourage 
going all cash. 
• 
Make sure you both are clear on how you will be billing them, whether it’s per lead, 
commission per sale, etc. 
• 
I bill my clients at the end of each month. If it is a commission deal, I follow up to 
confirm how many deals were made before invoicing them. If it is a per lead deal, I send 
them a screenshot of CallSling, along with the invoice. Finally, if it is a monthly flat rate, 
I just let Stripe automatically invoice them. 
 
Pro Tip: As soon as you are pulling in over $1k a month, start an LLC for yourself. This 
will not be covered in this course, but there are numerous tax benefits to doing so. I also 
recommend utilizing 1-800-Accountants for your tax time needs. In my opinion, it’s 
worth completely automating your tax and business services. I pay 1-800-accountants 
to take care of all my business filing and tax preparation needs. As a general rule of 
thumb, I set aside 30% of my monthly digital real estate income for taxes.

---

title: Kyle Adams - Value of Your Leads
content:
• 
Google “Your Niche average cost Your Area“ (i.e. Wedding DJ average cost Castle 
Rock) 
 
• 
So, on average, a booked wedding DJ can stand to make $1750 from my lead 
• 
A typical sales commission is 15% 
• 
1750x.15 = $262.50 per CLOSED lead 
• 
Note that not every lead you send them will result in a sale. 
• 
A SAFE typical closing rate estimate is 10% 
• 
262.50 x .10 = $26.25 
• 
Each lead you send your client is SAFELY estimated at $26.25 
Note that a SAFE lead estimate is your BOTTOM LINE. This is the lowest dollar amount 
your lead is worth. The closing rate on a warm lead (what you are sending them) is 
much higher. I typically estimate 25% for my closing estimate. 
Different Monetization Methods 
Commission Based 
• 
They pay you based on a predetermined percentage of the sale your lead created (i.e. a 
lead comes in, your client books a $500 dollar painting gig, they pay you the agreed 
15% commission equaling $75 bucks.) 
• 
Low risk for the business owner. 
• 
Easy to sell the idea. You win when they win. 
• 
Hard to track. You need to trust the business owner is being upfront with the cost of 
their business and paying you the agreed-upon amount. 
• 
The go-to when starting out. 
Pay Per Lead 
• 
Owner pays you a flat fee per lead that comes in, whether or not a sale is made. 
• 
This is how many of the large lead generation websites (thumbtack, home advisor, etc. 
work) 
• 
Easy to track. You charge them per lead that was sent to them from CallSling and 
Email. 
• 
I utilize the formula seen in “Value of your lead” to determine my pay per lead 
cost. (average price x sales commission)closing rate = price per lead 
Flat Monthly Fee 


• 
Owner pays you a flat monthly fee ($500-$10k+) regardless of how many leads come in 
• 
High risk to business owners. If tons of leads come in, they save money, if little to none 
come in they lose. 
• 
Difficult to sell. Approach this idea after doing business with the owner for a while, or if 
you are generating a high volume of leads coming in. 
Combination 
• 
A combo of any of the above methods (i.e. owner pays you a flat fee of $5 per lead and 
15% commission. Or, owner pays you a flat fee of $250 per month and 15% per 
successful job) 
• 
Typically transition into this, start with the commission and pitch this once you’ve 
brought them business 
 
As with any negotiating, have a number in mind. Start high and leave room to come 
down to your number. An easy way to do this is to pitch a combo idea “Let’s do a flat fee 
of $250 a month and a 20% commission on each job.” If they agree to this, you win, if 
not, pivot to “Alright, tell you what. Let’s start with the lowest risk to you – I’ll only win if 
you win. Let’s start with a flat 20% commission on any job my site brings you. We can 
reevaluate after I’ve proven myself”. 
 
Once you’ve valued your leads, you’re ready to start reaching out to 
business owners! Let’s move on

title: AI Content Scheduling Quickstart SOP
content:
FACEBOOK AI CONTENT SCHEDULER
QUICKSTART
SOP



Download Content Scheduler AI
Login: Your Email Address
Password: 123456  



Setting Up Content Scheduler AI

STEP 1: Grab Your Open AI API Key

Visit:  https://platform.openai.com/account/api-keys 

Sign in or sign up with your google/microsoft/apple account




Goto -> API Keys and create a new secret key



Copy & save it in a secure place (will only be displayed once)
Goto->billing and setup a payment method for api charges

PLEASE NOTE: THIS IS NOT THE SAME AS YOUR CHATGPT PLUS PAID ACCOUNT.  USAGE WILL ONLY COST YOU A FEW CENTS FOR THE API ACCESS.   




STEP 2: Integrate API into Content Scheduler AI
	
Download Content Scheduler AI (not yet live on chrome)
Open Content Scheduler AI -> Settings









Setup system prompt - this gives AI some content & role to play.  Set model at GPT 3.5 (GPT 4 currently has limitations).  Provide specifics on your business.

	For example: 

You are a (describe your business….eg. Facebook Social Media Coach/Health & Wellness Coach) that is trained on Russell Brunson and Sabri Suby's sales & marketing principles and helps (describe your target market….eg. men 35-45 looking to improve health and manage busy work-life balance) with (describe your products/services….eg. automation & lead generation).   





STEP 3: Setup Facebook links

Scroll to -> Facebook Link Settings and add all the Facebook profile & groups you want to post in from your personal profile.  You don’t need to be admin.  







Make sure to grab the entire Facebook URL of your personal profile or groups you want to post, and give it a name.  Add as many groups as you want to schedule posts in. 



Personal profile



Facebook groups



Add name & Facebook URL













STEP 4: Ready to start scheduling posts :) 

Hover to -> Scheduler on the main menu


Click on -> Scheduled Now



Click on -> Post Content and then -> AI Generated Content

(user generated content is still possible without AI) 

You should see the system prompt that you setup previously already written for you by default



Setup your AI prompt to start generating contents.  You should first do some research about your audience, and know everything about them.  








For Example, ask ChatGPT or your audience for research: 

List 10 core topics for growing a [NICHE] business, such as sales and marketing
List top 10 must read books for growing a [NICHE] business
List the top 10 influencers for the [NICHE] industry 
List the top 10 challenges in growing a [NICHE] business
List the top 10 pain points faced by the [NICHE] industry
List the top 10 [NICHE TOPIC] tips for the [NICHE] industry 


To get the best results, be specific with your AI prompts for your niche & the results or problems you’re trying to solve.  

You can use our built in Spintax to create variations of AI prompts, so that each AI generated contents are drawing from different sources.  

Once you’ve done your research, you can start inputting them into different prompts in Content Scheduler AI to generate content. 





You can get AI to:

TEACH FROM BOOKS
Randomly take a core topics from the book {BOOK 1|BOOK 2|BOOK 3|BOOK 4} and explain the concept in detail as if was your own.

QUICK TIP & HACK
Generate a random {topic 1|topic 2| topic 3| topic 4} tip for [NICHE] as inspiration.  

ASK A QUESTION
Ask a random question to [NICHE] about {topic 1|topic 2| topic 3| topic 4} to create engagements

LIFE LESSON
Create a random life lesson quote to inspire & motivate [NICHE] in your own words.  

MOTIVATIONAL QUOTE
Create a motivational quote from {person 1 | person 2 | person 3 | person 4 | person 5} and put it in your own words

RECOMMENDATIONS
Recommend a book that is worth reading for YOUR NICHE related to {topic 1|topic 2| topic 3| topic 4} and explain why

STORY POST
Based on the {Pain Point 1|Pain Point 2|Pain Point 3|Pain Point 4|} as a bottleneck in growing a [NICHE] business, create a story around it and offer a possible solution to the problem.  

VALUE POST
Based on {problem 1|problem 2|problem 3|problem 4|problem 5} faced by [NICHE] owners as a hurdle to their business, provide some insights as to how to overcome this problem to grow their business

TAKE A POLL
create a random business poll about 
{topic 1|topic 2| topic 3| topic 4} to get engagements from [NICHE] limited to 130 characters including spacing

AGREE OR DISAGREE?
Starting with "Agree or Disagree", write a random statement limited to 130 characters including spacing on the topic {topic 1|topic 2| topic 3| topic 4}

FUN FACT:
Starting with {Did you know:|Fun fact:|Interesting fact:|True stat:} give a random statistical fun fact about the topic {topic 1|topic 2| topic 3| topic 4}

BORROWED AUTHORITY POST
Explain why [NICHE] should follow sales & marketing principles from {Authority 1|Authority 2|Authority 3|Authority 4} in growing their business.

THINGS YOU SHOULD DO
Create a list of 5 things that [NICHE] owners should do now in {topic 1|topic 2| topic 3| topic 4}
 to grow their [NICHE] business


THINGS YOU SHOULD’T DO
Create a list of 5 things that [NICHE] owners should not do in {topic 1|topic 2| topic 3| topic 4} to avoid hurting their [NICHE] business

DID YOU KNOW
List out 5 random interesting facts about {topic 1|topic 2| topic 3| topic 4} that [NICHE] owners don’t already know

These are just some build blocks for you to start your AI Prompts to generate months of content in a few clicks.   

There are literally MILLIONS of possibilities with AI Prompts, and combined with SPINTAX feature will give you never-ending contents to schedule and post.  

Have fun, be creative, and think outside the box. Most important, be specific with your requests. 

You can easily enhance it by giving it more context or instructions.  The more information you provide, the better content results will be generated by AI.  

You can also enhance the prompts to:

Add an open-ended question that prompts engagements, deep thinking and comments
Explain how the content would relate and help grow [NICHE] business
Remove and words, books, authority, remove opening & closing quote marks " etc you don’t want
Speak in first person, third person or directly as if you are speaking to [NICHE] owners
Separate each sentence
Limit to 130 characters including spacing (for text background)
Start with “What do you think”, “I need your help”, “Here’s some quick tips” etc.  
Add relevant hashtags 


STEP 5: Generate AI image, upload or use text backgrounds 

The next step is to decide on what kind of images you want AI to display on your posts.  

You can also upload your own images if you want to personalize it, or select text backgrounds if you have less than 130 characters incl. spacing in your post (make sure toggle OFF “Generate AI Image”)

To use AI Image generator, simply toggle “Generate AI Image” to ON, type your query & decide on how many images you want to generate. 





Image Query Example:

INSPIRATION VIEWS
A person overlooking breathtaking views of {moutain | ocean | cliff | city | forest | waterfall | glacier }

BUSINESS MEETINGS
A group of salespeople {sitting | standing} around a table with laptops
Another way to generate the image prompts you’re looking for, is to reverse engineer from images you like, and find the relevant prompts relating to that image.  

To do this, firstly you want to search for an image on google.  

Ie. type in “handshake” on at http://images.google.com/ 

Pick the image style you like, and save the image.  

Then, upload the image into Clip Interrogator:

https://huggingface.co/spaces/pharma/CLIP-Interrogator 
 


Voila! There’s your prompt :)   



STEP 6: Schedule & repeat your prompts :) 

You’re now ready to start scheduling away months of contents!! 




Categorize your post by selecting a post type.  You can use one of our preset categories or create your own.  This will be useful for you to keep track of all the different types of contents you’ve created.  

Decide on date & time you want this to be posted. 

Optional tags if you want to customize your content categories even further.  

Decide on where you want to post.  You should have already setup all your Facebook links in the settings, if not, refer to step 3 above.  

You can select all groups/profiles at once or choose which ones you want to post in.  Remember, you don’t need to be admins to post, but you must have permissions to post. 





Next, this is where it gets interesting!  

Toggle on “Repeat Post” and you can have Content Scheduler AI run your prompts a dozen times, and schedule them in your calendar with just a few simple clicks.  

Remember, you should already have SPINTAX active so that the prompts will randomly select a different topic to talk about each time it runs it.  

So let’s say you want to schedule it every 7 days for the next 7 weeks, you would set it as:

Post frequency: 7 days
Random delay: 5-10 mins (to avoid being seen as a bot)
Number of post: 6 (the current generated post + 6 more)

Then, you have the option to set an automated first comment.  

This is particularly useful to encourage comments, engagements or place your link.

For example:

Would love to hear your thoughts! 
Want to know more? Join our Facebook Group! 
Here’s the download link to the lead magnet 
Here’s the link to register for the upcoming challenge
Here’s a demo video to see you can also scheduler months of contents with AI on your personal profile! 



Once you’re all done with the above, simply click NEXT and then ADD.

title: Review Management Quick Start Guide
content:
Quick Start Guide









Step 1:  Updating Password






Changing your password helps to keep your account safe.

As soon as you sign in using the credentials sent with the welcome email, you should change the initial password by clicking Settings on the left sidebar.




Then click Account.




You’ll be presented with the field to enter your New Password.




Repeat the same password in the Confirm Password field.




***  Just below the Confirm Password field, you can go to the Choose Language field, and change from English to your native language (23 different languages available).

Finally, click Save.

Keep your password safe. Moving forward, you’ll use your new password to log into your account.
















Step 2:  Integrations




After updating the account password, the next step to take is integrating review platforms.

To do so, firstly click Settings in the left sidebar.  Then click Integrations.





We can integrate over twenty different review platforms. To connect your platform, simply click on Integrate below each platform.




After you click on the Integrate button, you will see on-screen instructions, guiding you through the platform integrating process.

For instance, the following picture shows the integration instructions of the Facebook page.










Step 3:  Reviews






After you have finished integrating all of your online review channels into your software platform, the “Reviews” section will automatically start to populate with all of your business’s reviews (this process could take some time).

In the Reviews section, you will see the list of integrated platforms with rating and number of reviews.

On the right sidebar, you can search and find reviews by entering keywords, filter reviews by Assessment, Type, Answer and Date.




You also have the opportunity to monitor and respond to all reviews right from your dashboard.
To do so, just go ahead and click Reply under each review.

















Step 4:  Using AI To Reply


We use the latest AI technology to generate AI replies for your reviews, which ultimately saves you time. To use this feature - simply click on AI Reply. Dr. Reviews intuitive AI feature automatically generates a reply to your reviewer

If you want AI to generate a new response, simply click the little “reload” button. AND, feel free to edit the responses if you wish, 







Step 5:  Review Link

Review Link is the link you’ll send out to your customers/patients. You can customize the review link by clicking Review Link on the left sidebar.






Here from Review Link, you can also edit the URL, as well as the link preview title.





Click on Desktop Image and a little edit icon (red pencil) appears on the right of each field in the desktop image of your review link. 




Here, you can also edit the image of the desktop image for your review link (Image size must be 720 px x 900 px)





Click Platform Choice to enable or disable platforms you’d like to showcase on the Review Link.

Once you choose the platforms of your choice, make sure to save changes by clicking the green checkmark button.





The Negative Feedback page is what your customers/patients see when they want to write a
1, 2 or 3-star review. Their negative review is sent directly to you ONLY for follow-up, in order to answer their concerns privately, while also protecting your high average star rating online. Low ratings affect your online reputation and lowers conversion rates of future prospects turning into customers/patients!  Think about your own buying decisions online when you compare the ratings of vendors you wish to buy a product or service from.  You always choose the provider with a higher rating, and so too will your customers/patients!






If your customers/patients want to write a 4 or 5-star review, a Negative Feedback page will not be presented to them.
Showing a Negative Feedback page in order to isolate negative feedback is optional, and you can disable it by clicking Review Link and choose Star filter disabled.




The Private Feedback section shows the page your customers/patients see when they want you to leave private feedback for you.




Customers/Patients can leave private feedback/rate their recent experience by choosing 1 to 5 stars, and then can click Send.
















Step 6:  Request Reviews






You can request reviews by clicking Get Reviews on the left sidebar and start requesting reviews via SMS, Email, WhatsApp and QR Code.

To send review requests, you can Upload a CSV file or invite customers/patients individually by entering Name and Number/Email manually (to add a new customer/patient, click “Add line”).

Make sure you have consent to send messages. Check I have consent to send messages to this contact and then click Request a review.






Once you are in the Get Reviews section, just scroll down, and you can customize your review request templates for SMS, Email and WhatsApp.

You can optionally send an automatic SMS/Email reminder after 3 and/or 7 days if the customer/patient doesn’t leave a review on the initial request. 

You also have the option to customize your SMS/Email reminder templates.



















Step 7:  Website Widgets






We have five types of website widgets (Carousel, Feed, Video, Pop-up, Badge) showcasing 4 and 5-star reviews with text on your website.





You can scroll down and further customize website widgets using different filters: Remove “Verified By” on the website widget, Dark mode, Hide scores of the integrated review platforms, Hide profile photos of the reviewers, Hide “leave a review” of the website widget.

After choosing your favorite website widget type with customization, simply copy the HTML code and paste it on your website.











Step 8:  Social Sharing



With the social sharing feature, you can connect your Facebook/Instagram accounts and customize the post template.




Now you can start sharing reviews by clicking Share on Social under each review.











Step 9:  Analytics






The Analytics section enables you the opportunity to visualize your growth with charts, populated with data that’s updated once a day.

The Analytics section shows the number of New Public Reviews; Reviews Growth chart, reflects the number of reviews you’ve received over time; Average Rating Growth shows you the average star rating of your reviews over time, on a scale of 1 to 5.

The Reviews Funnel consists of:

Invites Sent: Total number of email, SMS and WhatsApp campaigns sent in the selected time frame.

Total Unique Visits: The total number of visits to your Review Link in the selected time frame.

QR Code Unique Visits: the total number of visits to the Review Link deriving from the QR code in the selected time frame.

New Public Reviews: reviews on public platforms (e.g. Google) in the selected time frame. Video reviews and private reviews are excluded.

Negative Feedback: private reviews resulting from negative customer/patient experiences in the selected time frame.
















Step 10:  Notifications





To receive email notifications for new reviews, please go to Settings, then click Notifications.

Make sure Notifications toggle is on. Then go ahead and enter your email in the following fields: 
Email for notifications and Email for replies to review requests.





















Step 11:  SMS Service






SMS Service enables you to connect your own messaging provider to send SMS review requests. If you are sending SMS to United States numbers, your connected phone must have an approved 10DLC or toll-free campaign.

To access SMS service, click Settings → SMS Service

Set up Twilio by entering Account SID and Auth Token. Once you’re done, click on Save.




You can also set up Telnyx by entering the Api Key, and then click on Save.

title: Telnyx Quick Start Guide
content:
Sign Up for a Telnyx Account:
Go to Telnyx Sign Up and create a new account.
Complete the account verification process as required.
Add Funds to the Account:
Prepay by adding funds to the account to purchase a Polish phone number and use SMS services. Add Funds
Purchase a Polish Mobile Phone Number:
Review the Poland DID Requirements to understand the documentation needed for personal or business identity verification and address verification.
Purchase a Polish mobile phone number that supports SMS through the Telnyx Mission Control Portal (as this is required for WhatsApp Business verification).
Choose a Pricing Plan for SMS:
Select the pay-as-you-go billing option, which allows for flexibility and payment only for the services used.
Configure auto payments in the Telnyx portal to ensure uninterrupted service. This can be done by adding a payment method and enabling automatic billing to handle charges as they occur.
Create and Configure a Messaging Profile:
Set up a Messaging Profile to manage SMS settings. Follow the guide on Setting Up a Messaging Profile.
Click on "Add new profile" and configure the messaging settings according to their needs.
Assign the purchased Polish phone number to the newly created Messaging Profile.
Link the Number to the Messaging Profile: 
Navigate to the "Numbers" section of the Telnyx portal.
Select the number they have purchased.
Assign the number to the messaging profile by selecting it from the dropdown menu.
Generate an API Key:
Once logged in, navigate to the API Keys section of the Telnyx portal.
Ensure that "API V2" is selected in the horizontal menu bar.
Click "Create API key".
Copy the API key and save it somewhere secure.
Test the Number:
Ensure that the Polish mobile phone number can send and receive SMS messages as expected by conducting test messages.
 (Optional) Webhooks:
If you want to receive real-time notifications for events such as incoming messages or delivery statuses, set up webhooks. Learn how to leverage webhooks with the guide on How to Leverage Webhooks.
Integrate with the Telnyx API (contact us here): 
Use the API key to authenticate API requests when integrating the reputation management system with the Telnyx Messaging API.
They can find detailed API documentation and code examples at Telnyx Developers.
Test the Integration: 
Before going live, you should test the integration to ensure that the API is working correctly and that they can send and receive SMS messages as expected.
(Optional) Verify Number with WhatsApp Business: 
Register the Polish mobile number with WhatsApp Business and complete the verification process using the SMS or call option.

title: Untitled
content:
# Google Docs Formatting Preferences

The standard for any one-off Google Doc created for Online Optimisers. Every doc should look clean, professional, and easy to scan. Headers navigate, callouts highlight, and white space breathes.

---

title: Page Setup
content:
- Size: A4
- Margins: 2cm top/bottom, 2.5cm left/right (tighter than default — maximizes content space)
- Page color: white
- Header/footer: none on internal docs; include doc title + date on client-facing docs

---

title: Fonts
content:
| Element | Font | Size | Weight |
|---------|------|------|--------|
| H1 (doc title) | Inter (Arial fallback) | 20pt | Bold |
| H2 (major section) | Inter | 14pt | Bold |
| H3 (subsection) | Inter | 12pt | Bold |
| Body text | Inter | 10.5pt | Regular |
| Table header | Inter | 10pt | Bold |
| Table body | Inter | 10pt | Regular |
| Caption / label | Inter | 9pt | Italic |
| Code / command | Courier New | 10pt | Regular |

---

title: Color Palette (matches Sheet palette)
content:
| Name | Hex | Use |
|------|-----|-----|
| Navy | #061B3F | H1 text, table headers (background) |
| Mid-navy | #253571 | H2 text |
| Dark gray | #1A1D26 | Body text |
| Accent blue | #3498DB | Links, highlights, H3 color (optional) |
| Amber light | #FFF3CC | Warning / critical callout box |
| Green light | #E0FAE9 | Success / win callout box |
| Blue light | #D1EAF9 | Info / note callout box |
| Cream | #FFFFF8 | Light aside or secondary info box |
| Light gray | #F5F7FA | Subtle section divider row in tables |

---

title: Headings
content:
- H1: Navy (#061B3F), 20pt bold, centered, space below 12pt
- H2: Mid-navy (#253571), 14pt bold, left-aligned, border bottom (1pt, navy), space above 16pt / below 8pt
- H3: Dark gray (#1A1D26), 12pt bold, left-aligned, space above 10pt / below 4pt
- Never skip heading levels (H1 > H2 > H3, not H1 > H3)

---

title: Body Text
content:
- 10.5pt Inter, dark gray (#1A1D26)
- Line spacing: 1.15
- Paragraph spacing: 6pt after each paragraph
- No first-line indent — use paragraph spacing for separation

---

title: Tables
content:
- Header row: navy background (#061B3F), white bold text, 10pt, center-aligned
- Body rows: alternating white / light gray (#F5F7FA)
- Cell padding: 6px all sides
- Borders: light gray (#CCCCCC), 0.5pt — subtle, not heavy
- First column: often bold (label/identifier column)
- No merged cells unless it is a title row — merged cells in tables break scanning

---

title: Callout Boxes
content:
Use a 1-column table with no visible border and a background color:

| Type | Background | Icon / label | Use |
|------|------------|--------------|-----|
| Warning / Critical | Amber #FFF3CC | ⚠ IMPORTANT | Blockers, risks, must-read |
| Win / Positive | Green #E0FAE9 | ✓ WIN | Good news, confirmed results |
| Info / Note | Blue #D1EAF9 | ℹ NOTE | Context, links, background info |
| Action required | Orange #FDEBD0 | → ACTION | Next step for Donal or Tomas |

Bold the label. Keep callout text short — 1-3 lines max.

---

title: Lists
content:
- Use bullet lists for non-ordered items (checkmarks for completed items, dashes for neutral)
- Use numbered lists for steps / sequences
- Indent sub-items once — avoid 3+ levels of nesting
- Keep list items short: one idea per bullet

---

title: Section Dividers
content:
- Use H2 heading as the primary visual break (with bottom border)
- For lighter separation: insert a 1pt horizontal line (Insert > Horizontal line)
- Never use a row of dashes or asterisks as a divider

---

title: Document Structure (standard for most internal docs)
content:
1. **Title block** (H1 + subtitle in body italic + date)
2. **TL;DR / Summary** (3-5 bullets — always first, before detail)
3. **Main sections** (H2 headings, numbered if sequential)
4. **Next steps / Actions** (explicit, owner named if known)
5. **Appendix** (optional — raw data, reference links, full transcripts)

---

title: OO Logo Files
content:
Search by filename within `source-docs/` — do NOT hardcode the full path (folder may be reorganised):

- **Transparent background (preferred):** `OP - Web Dev Logo - transparent - cropped.png` — use on any background color
- **White background:** `OP - Web Dev Logo - white background - cropped.png` — use on white doc headers only

To find: `Glob("**/OP - Web Dev Logo - transparent - cropped.png", path="source-docs")` will return the current location regardless of subfolder.

---

title: Client-Facing Docs
content:
When a doc will be shared with a client or prospect:

- **Cover page:** OO logo top-left (`OP - Web Dev Logo - transparent - cropped.png`), client name, doc title, date, "Prepared by Online Optimisers" line
- Logo sizing on cover: ~5cm wide, left-aligned, top of page before the title block
- Logo in header (non-cover pages): small (~2cm wide), top-left corner of page header
- Use the OO brand colors (navy + accent blue) throughout
- Remove any internal notes, internal links, or placeholder text before sharing
- Change page margins to 3cm left/right for a cleaner reading layout
- Footer: "Prepared by Online Optimisers | info@onlineoptimisers.com.au" (small, gray, centered)

---

title: Internal Docs
content:
- No cover page needed
- Keep it lean — the TL;DR block replaces a long intro
- Status indicator at top: 🟢 Final / 🟡 Draft / 🔴 Needs Review
- Add last-edited date in the subtitle line

---

title: Links
content:
- Hyperlink with descriptive anchor text — never paste raw URLs in body text
- Exception: reference sections / appendix where the raw URL is useful to copy
- Link color: accent blue (#3498DB), underlined (default Docs behavior)

---

title: Images / Screenshots
content:
- Center-align all images
- Caption below (9pt italic, dark gray) describing what the image shows
- Max width: full column width (do not let images float or wrap text)
- Compress before inserting if over 1MB

---

title: Naming Convention
content:
`[Topic] - [Client or Internal] - [Date YYYY-MM-DD]`

Examples:
- `AI Ranking Audit - Bobcat Movers - 2026-04-07`
- `Monthly Report Brief - Internal - 2026-04`
- `Proposal - Handyman Superior - 2026-03-25`

title: Current ICP
content:
- US service businesses
- Especially moving companies — strongest current niche
- Also: plumbers, roofers, dentists, metal-related businesses, similar local operators
- Owner-led or small team
- Local or regional operator (not national)
- Cares about calls, leads, Maps visibility, and local keyword rankings
- Can afford ~$1,500/month+
- Not starting from zero — has some existing web presence

title: Strongest-fit signals
content:
- Has some cash and is not in severe distress
- Already has Google reviews (some traction)
- Has enough existing visibility that better local SEO can materially grow the business
- Can benefit from stronger GBP + website lead gen working together

title: Weak-fit signals
content:
- No website or brand presence at all
- Cannot afford the retainer
- Wants guaranteed ranking results in unrealistic timeframes
- Already working with a strong in-house SEO team

title: Sales channels currently in use
content:
- Cold email (via Instantly, testing Bison)
- LinkedIn outreach (3 accounts)
- Niche-specific VSL outreach
- Warm calling after email or LinkedIn interest
- Occasional cold calling

title: Claude + AI Setup Tips (from Neale + Tony, 2026-03-15)
content:
- Git first — always initialise git before starting a project
- Create log.md per project — tracks decisions, changes, context between sessions
- BMAD Method — spec-first agent framework, use when building AI delivery systems
- Spec kit — define inputs, outputs, acceptance criteria before building anything
- Build new sites on Astro, not WordPress — faster, markdown-friendly, easier for Claude
- "I'm the ideas person, Claude is the execution expert" — push back on bad calls

title: Revenue Model Insights (2026-03-15)
content:
- Actual MRR: $13,194 (not $14-15k as estimated)
- Moving Pro Inc at $500 — underpriced, low margin (40%), worth restructuring
- Maestro Movers at $750 — underpriced, was flagged pink in Jan (late pay risk)
- Elite Movers + Soft Touch Moving = best upsell targets for AI ranking (highest value, best margin)
- 5 of 9 clients are movers — niche is real, lean into it
- Path to $100k/month requires ~23 new clients + upsells over 6 months
- All-in $5k package (SEO + AI ranking + FB ads + nurturing) carries months 5-6 — must be delivery-ready by month 4

title: Strategic Reminders
content:
- Proof before expansion — don't sell FB ads or new services before delivery is proven
- Upsell AI ranking to existing clients first before chasing new ones
- Donal is the bottleneck — every automation that removes him from the flow increases capacity
